title: 2 Corinthians 10
subtitle: Paul’s Defence of His Authority

heb.paragraph: The noun logismos (argument, reasoning) in v. 5 denotes the intellectual fortifications—ideological systems, philosophical presuppositions, cultural assumptions—that resist the knowledge of God. Paul’s warfare is not against people but against ideas: he ‘demolishes arguments’ (logismous) and ‘every pretension that sets itself up against the knowledge of God.’ The military metaphor is sustained: arguments are strongholds (ochyrōmata, v. 4), and every thought is taken ‘captive’ (aichmalōtizō) to Christ.

The adjective dynatos (powerful) in v. 4 describes the apostolic weapons as having ‘divine power’ (dynata tō theō). The dative tō theō could be instrumental (‘powerful through God’) or dative of reference (‘powerful before God’). Either way, the weapons’ power is divine in origin, not human. The contrast with ‘weapons of the world’ (hopla sarkika) establishes that apostolic authority operates on a fundamentally different plane from worldly power.

refs.note: Paul’s spiritual-warfare imagery in Ephesians provides the fuller development of the metaphor introduced here: the armour of God, the sword of the Spirit, and the struggle against spiritual powers.

Paul’s earlier disclaimer—‘My message and my preaching were not with wise and persuasive words, but with a demonstration of the Spirit’s power’—provides the theological rationale for the non-worldly weapons described here.

notes.note: Paul’s opponents have caricatured him as a coward: bold in letters, timid in person. Paul’s response appeals to ‘the humility and gentleness of Christ’ (v. 1)—his manner reflects Christ’s character, not personal weakness. He warns that he is prepared to be ‘bold’ (tolmēsai) in person if necessary.

The spiritual-warfare passage distinguishes apostolic ministry from worldly power. Paul’s weapons are not rhetorical skill, social status, or physical force but the ‘divine power to demolish strongholds’ (v. 4). The strongholds are intellectual: arguments and pretensions that resist God’s truth. The goal is not destruction but subjection: every thought brought ‘captive to Christ’ (v. 5).

Calvin reads the spiritual-warfare passage as definitive for the nature of ministerial authority. The minister’s power is not coercive but persuasive, not worldly but spiritual. The ‘strongholds’ demolished are false doctrines and sinful reasonings that resist the gospel. Calvin applies this to theological controversy: the church’s weapons against heresy are not political power but the truth of Scripture wielded in the Spirit’s power.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note discusses the ‘strongholds’ (ochyrōmata): the term is military, denoting a fortified position. Paul uses it metaphorically for the intellectual and spiritual barriers that prevent people from accepting the gospel. The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

fee.source: Gordon D. Fee, Pauline Christology (2007) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Fee reads the tonal shift of ch. 10 as Paul’s direct engagement with the super-apostles who have been undermining his authority. Fee argues that Paul’s spiritual-warfare imagery is not merely metaphorical but reflects his genuine belief in a cosmic conflict between the gospel and the powers of the present evil age.

harris.source: Murray J. Harris, 2 Corinthians, NIGTC/EGC (2005) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Harris provides the most balanced discussion of the literary-critical question (are chs. 10–13 a separate letter?), cautiously defending the canonical sequence while acknowledging the tonal shift. He reads the spiritual-warfare passage as Paul’s programmatic response to opponents who evaluate ministry by worldly criteria: Paul’s weapons operate on a plane the super-apostles cannot comprehend.

hist.context: Chapter 10 marks a dramatic tonal shift: the conciliatory, affectionate tone of chs. 1–9 gives way to sharp polemic against opponents who challenge Paul’s authority. Many scholars see chs. 10–13 as a separate letter (the ‘tearful letter’ or a later addition), though others defend the canonical sequence. Paul’s opponents have charged him with being ‘timid when face to face’ but ‘bold when away’ (v. 1)—an accusation of inconsistency. Paul’s response reframes the issue: his ‘weapons’ are not worldly but divinely powerful, and his authority is exercised not through personal impressiveness but through the Spirit’s power to demolish ideological strongholds.

heb.paragraph: The noun oikodomē (building up) in v. 8 describes the purpose of apostolic authority: ‘for building you up rather than tearing you down.’ The constructive metaphor is fundamental to Paul’s self-understanding: his authority exists not for self-aggrandisement but for the community’s edification. The same term appears in 13:10, forming an inclusio around the polemical section.

refs.note: Paul’s earlier acknowledgement that he came to Corinth ‘not with eloquence or human wisdom’ provides the theological context for the opponents’ critique of his rhetorical ability.
The building-up/tearing-down language of 10:8 recurs at the letter’s close (13:10), forming an inclusio around the polemical section.

notes.note: Paul insists that his opponents ‘belong to Christ’ no more than he does (v. 7). His authority, given by the Lord, is for construction, not destruction (v. 8)—a claim the super-apostles cannot make, since their ministry is dividing the church.

Paul warns his critics: the authority displayed in his letters will be equally present in person (v. 11). The charge that his letters are strong but his presence weak will be tested at the upcoming visit.

Calvin reads Paul’s response as a refusal to compete on worldly terms. The minister’s authority does not rest on personal charisma but on divine commission. Calvin warns against a church culture that selects leaders based on eloquence and appearance rather than faithfulness and sound doctrine.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on Paul’s physical presence discusses the ancient biographical tradition: some later texts (Acts of Paul and Thecla) describe Paul as short, bald, and bow-legged, though the historical reliability of this description is uncertain. The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

fee.source: Gordon D. Fee, Pauline Christology (2007) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Fee argues that the opponents’ critique reflects the Corinthian obsession with sophistic rhetorical performance. Paul’s refusal to adopt the rhetorical conventions of the Second Sophistic is not a deficiency but a deliberate theological choice: the gospel cannot be reduced to persuasive technique.

harris.source: Murray J. Harris, 2 Corinthians, NIGTC/EGC (2005) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Harris reads Paul’s self-defence as strategically restrained: he claims authority without displaying it (yet). The warning of v. 11—what he is in letters, he will be in person—is a promissory threat whose fulfilment depends on the Corinthians’ response.

hist.context: Paul addresses the charge that his letters are ‘weighty and forceful’ but his personal presence is ‘unimpressive’ and his speaking ‘amounts to nothing’ (v. 10). This is one of the few biographical details about Paul’s physical appearance and rhetorical ability in the NT. The Corinthians, formed in a culture that prized rhetorical brilliance and physical presence, found Paul lacking. Paul’s response is not to deny the charge but to redirect the criterion: authority is measured by divine assignment and edifying purpose, not by personal impressiveness.

heb.paragraph: The noun kanōn (rule, standard, sphere) in vv. 13, 15, 16 denotes the divinely assigned sphere of apostolic operation. Paul’s ‘boasting’ is confined to the territory God has assigned him—which includes Corinth, since Paul was the founding apostle. The super-apostles, by contrast, boast about ‘work done by others’ (v. 15)—they have invaded Paul’s territory and claim credit for his founding labour.

refs.note: Paul’s citation of ‘Let the one who boasts boast in the Lord’ draws on Jeremiah’s repudiation of boasting in wisdom, strength, or riches. The same citation appears in 1 Cor 1:31.
Paul’s commitment to preaching ‘where Christ was not known’ and not building ‘on someone else’s foundation’ parallels the sphere-of-activity argument here.

notes.note: Paul’s critique is devastating: the super-apostles ‘measure themselves by themselves’ (v. 12)—a circular evaluation that proves nothing. Paul’s boasting is limited to his God-assigned sphere, which includes Corinth because he was the founding apostle.

Paul’s ambition is not to boast about others’ work but to preach the gospel ‘in the regions beyond’ (v. 16)—his pioneering missionary vision. The Jeremiah citation (v. 17) provides the theological criterion: all legitimate boasting centres on the Lord’s activity, not human achievement. The final statement (v. 18) is definitive: self-commendation means nothing; what matters is the Lord’s commendation.

Calvin reads the sphere-of-activity argument as a principle of ministerial accountability: each minister is responsible for the territory God has assigned. Invading another’s territory and claiming credit for their work is a form of theft. The Jeremiah citation grounds all boasting in God’s activity: the minister who boasts must boast only in what the Lord has done through him.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on kanōn explains its range: the term can mean a measuring rod, a rule or standard, or a sphere of activity. In this context, it refers to the divinely assigned territory of Paul’s apostolic mission. The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

fee.source: Gordon D. Fee, Pauline Christology (2007) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Fee reads the sphere-of-activity argument as Paul’s definitive response to the super-apostles’ territorial intrusion. They have arrived in Paul’s church and are claiming credit for spiritual realities that Paul’s founding ministry produced. Fee notes that Paul’s missionary vision (v. 16) extends beyond Corinth to ‘regions beyond’—evidence that Paul’s ambition is always forward-looking, not territorial.

harris.source: Murray J. Harris, 2 Corinthians, NIGTC/EGC (2005) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Harris provides the most detailed analysis of the kanōn concept, arguing that Paul understands his apostolic sphere as divinely apportioned rather than self-chosen. The super-apostles’ error is not merely boasting but boasting outside their assigned area—a violation of the divine order of ministry. Harris reads v. 18 as the chapter’s theological climax: the ultimate validation of ministry comes not from self-assessment or peer comparison but from the Lord’s own commendation.

hist.context: Paul contrasts his own ‘measured’ boasting with the super-apostles’ self-promotion. They ‘commend themselves’ and ‘measure themselves by themselves’ (v. 12)—a circular, self-referential evaluation that Paul dismisses as foolish. His own boasting is confined to his God-assigned sphere (kanōn), which includes Corinth since he founded the church there. The Jer 9:24 citation (v. 17: ‘Let the one who boasts boast in the Lord’) provides the definitive principle: all legitimate boasting is theocentric, directed toward the Lord’s work rather than human achievement.

rows.label: vv.1–2
rows.text: Paul’s appeal: humility mistaken for timidity

rows.label: vv.3–6
rows.text: Spiritual warfare: divine weapons, demolishing arguments

rows.label: vv.7–11
rows.text: Authority for building up: letters and presence

rows.label: vv.15–18
rows.text: Boasting in the Lord: Jeremiah’s criterion

lit.note: Chapter 10 opens the polemical section (chs. 10–13) with a defence of apostolic authority grounded in spiritual warfare, divine assignment, and theocentric boasting.

themes.note: Mission peaks as Paul defends his pioneering apostolic sphere and envisions preaching in regions beyond Corinth. Sovereignty scores highly as divine assignment governs ministerial authority.

ppl.name: Paul
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

thread.text: Paul’s spiritual-warfare imagery in Ephesians provides the fuller development of the metaphor introduced here: the armour of God, the sword of the Spirit, and the struggle against spiritual powers.

Paul’s earlier disclaimer—‘My message and my preaching were not with wise and persuasive words, but with a demonstration of the Spirit’s power’—provides the theological rationale for the non-worldly weapons described here.
Paul’s earlier acknowledgement that he came to Corinth ‘not with eloquence or human wisdom’ provides the theological context for the opponents’ critique of his rhetorical ability.
The building-up/tearing-down language of 10:8 recurs at the letter’s close (13:10), forming an inclusio around the polemical section.

discourse.thesis: Paul defends his apostolic authority against those who accuse him of being bold in letters but weak in person.

nodes.text: Paul appeals by the meekness and gentleness of Christ — accused of being humble in person but bold when away. He begs not to be bold when present.
We walk in the flesh but do not wage war according to the flesh. Our weapons have divine power to destroy strongholds and every lofty opinion raised against knowledge of God.

Look at what is before your eyes. If anyone is confident he belongs to Christ, so do we. Paul's letters are weighty, but in person he is weak? Let such people know: what we say by letter, we will do when present.
We do not compare ourselves with those who commend themselves. We boast within limits — the field God assigned. Let the one who boasts, boast in the Lord.

coaching.tip: 'The weapons of our warfare are not of the flesh but have divine power to destroy strongholds' (v. 4). Paul's opponents mocked his weak presence (v. 10). He admits bodily weakness but claims spiritual power. The battle is ideological — 'arguments and every lofty opinion raised against the knowledge of God' (v. 5). Truth is the weapon.

'Let the one who boasts, boast in the Lord' (v. 17, quoting Jer 9:24). Self-commendation is worthless; divine commendation matters. Paul's opponents paraded credentials. Paul will too (chapters 11-12) — but reluctantly, sarcastically, pointing beyond himself. Boasting in the Lord means boasting in what the Lord did through weakness.

prayer_prompt: As Paul says 'we demolish arguments and every pretension that sets itself up against the knowledge of God,' consider asking God what mental stronghold — a pattern of thinking, a false belief about yourself — needs to be taken captive to Christ.
